planting.note: Set out container plants or nursery divisions in spring after the ground is workable. Cold stratification of seeds for 4-6 weeks improves germination rates.

directions: New Mexico Checker-mallow is a native perennial of moist mountain meadows, stream banks, and valley grasslands throughout the southwestern US and Great Basin, thriving across New Mexico, Colorado, Utah, and Arizona. It produces slender, upright spikes of bright rose-pink to lavender-pink mallow flowers from June through August, adding long-lasting color to summer gardens. Plant in well-drained, moderately moist soil in full sun; once established it tolerates periods of dryness better than most other Sidalcea species. Grow from container plants or nursery-propagated divisions for best results, as transplants establish more reliably than direct-seeded plants in garden settings.
sources: Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center Plant Database

planting.note: Plant container-grown specimens in spring in moist, well-drained soil. Soak seeds in near-boiling water for 24 hours before sowing to scarify the hard seed coat and significantly improve germination.

directions: Streambank Wild Hollyhock is a bold, shrubby native perennial found along rocky stream banks, moist meadows, and avalanche tracks in the Rocky Mountains from British Columbia south to New Mexico. It grows into impressive, large-leaved plants with deeply lobed, maplelike foliage topped by tall spikes of rose-pink to lavender flowers 2-3 inches across from June through August. Plant in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; it thrives in garden sites that mimic its native riparian habitat and benefits from consistent moisture through the growing season. Soak seeds in near-boiling water for 24 hours before sowing to break dormancy, and expect plants to reach full flowering size in their second or third year.

planting.note: Plant in spring after frost danger has passed in consistently moist to wet soil. Nick or soak seeds in warm water overnight before sowing to improve germination speed and uniformity.

directions: Hairy Rose Mallow is a native perennial of river banks, wetland margins, and moist open habitats from Virginia to Florida and Texas, bearing enormous flowers 4-6 inches across in white to pale pink with a deep maroon eye from June through September. Its stout stems and broadly lobed leaves are covered in dense, soft, velvety hairs that distinguish it from the closely related Hibiscus moscheutos. Grow in consistently moist to wet, slightly acidic to neutral, rich soil in full sun; it is ideally suited for rain gardens, pond edges, and wetland restoration sites where the soil stays reliably moist or intermittently flooded. Plants die back to the ground each winter and re-emerge reliably in spring, growing quickly to flowering size by midsummer.

planting.note: Plant in spring once soils have warmed in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty soil in full sun. In zones 7-8 provide a light mulch over the root crown in winter for added protection.

directions: Comfortroot is a native Hibiscus of wet longleaf pine flatwoods, bogs, and seasonal wetlands along the southeastern coastal plain from North Carolina to Florida, bearing cream-white to pale yellow flowers with a striking dark maroon eye from July through September. Its deeply lobed, palmate leaves give it a distinctive texture among native mallows, and its smaller stature compared to showier rose mallows makes it useful in tighter planting schemes. Grow in moist to wet, acidic, sandy or peaty, nutrient-poor soil in full sun, mimicking the conditions of its native longleaf pine savanna habitat. An excellent, low-maintenance native for Gulf Coast landscapes, rain gardens, and longleaf pine ecosystem restorations where most ornamentals would struggle.

planting.note: Plant in spring in very well-drained, dry, calcareous or rocky soil in full sun. Avoid any soil amendment with organic matter; this plant demands lean, dry conditions to thrive and bloom freely.

directions: Chihuahuan Globe Mallow is a compact, velvety-soft native mallow shrub of the Chihuahuan Desert and adjacent Texas Hill Country, its stems and leaves coated in dense, silky white hairs that give the plant a striking silver-gray appearance year-round. From summer through fall it produces cheerful, bright yellow, five-petaled mallow flowers about 1-1.5 inches across, the golden blooms providing a vivid contrast against the silver foliage. Plant in very dry, poor, calcareous or rocky, well-drained soil in full sun; wet or clay-heavy soils quickly lead to root rot, so excellent drainage is non-negotiable. Once established it requires minimal supplemental irrigation and is an outstanding choice for Texas xeriscape designs, Chihuahuan Desert gardens, and other arid Southwest landscapes.
sources: Texas Native Plants Database - Lady Bird Johnson Wildflower Center
